text: Tips and tricks of minimally invasive approach of complicated ileocecal Crohn's disease.

Prior to the operative approach to these patients, surgeons must perform a correct staging of the disease, performing a video colonoscopy with exploration of the terminal ileum and imaging studies to explore the small intestine looking for stenosis or fistulas.
This way, the surgeon will be able to obtain information to plan the surgical procedure before going to the operating room.
Among the specific studies to study the small intestine, we will find the capsule endoscopy.

When requesting the study in patients with Crohn's disease, we must bear in mind that in those with the stenosing pattern, the capsule may be returned proximal to the stenosis, which could result in an indication for emergency surgery.
During the preoperative evaluation, it should be taken into account that these patients often have severe nutritional deficiencies that require intensive nutrition prior to surgery.
For those patients who are receiving chronic steroids, a reduction prior to surgery would help to reduce the mortality of the procedure.
Finally, we must remember that the evidence has shown that perioperative treatment with biological drugs does not increase the complication of surgical treatment.

Regarding the minimally invasive approach in complicated Crohn's disease, several publications have shown that it is feasible for these patients improving operative results compared to the conventional approach.
The use of medial approach to mobilize the right colon is often difficult in these patients, especially in those who present a fistulizing pattern generating inflammatory tumors
that involve different parts of the small intestine and colon, preventing the correct visualization of the dissection planes by this way.
It is not uncommon to see firmly adhered bowel loops with intermediate abscesses that are unblocked when attempting to release those additions.
For all of the above, in patients with complicated Crohn's disease, we suggest mobilizing the right colon using a lateral approach, a plan that is oftenly respected in these patients.
The surgeon carefully identifies the duodenum, which is visually attached to the posterior aspect of the inflammatory tumor in patients with fistulizing Crohn's disease.
Regardless of this advice, surgeons must be ready to use combined approaches if the complexity of the case requires it.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the inflammatory tumor by opening and dissecting the mesentery, liberating the terminal ileum and cecum, and fully mobilizing the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon performs end-block exteriorization of the specimen through a midline incision, revealing the excised tissue.
When choosing the place to perform the mini laparotomy, we suggest performing it in the midline, in order to protect sides of the possible future ostomies.
The video shows the placement of ostomies through the midline abdominal wall, highlighting the surgical approach for ostomy creation.
Following the completion of the dissection and mini laparotomy, the surgeon prepares to perform the NM block exteriorization of the specimen through the midline incision.
The surgeon lengthens the diameter of the midline incision to accommodate the large inflammatory tumor, which involves multiple bowel segments.

Small bowel semiology should be performed routinely in these patients either laparoscopically or after performing the mini-laparotomy in search of complications of the disease in the proximal intestine even when these have not been diagnosed in preoperative images.
The surgeon performs ligation of the thickened intestinal mesentery, which is visibly affected by the disease, highlighting the increased risk of postoperative bleeding at this level.
For this reason, we recommend that the ligation is carried out extracorporally with reabsorbable stitches.
The surgeon prepares to perform a primary anastomosis in lateral fashion using a surgical stapler, creating a wide mouth to potentially reduce the risk of postoperative stenosis.
A new alternative that is gaining popularity these days is the CONOS-S technique, which exceeds the purpose of this video.

Those cases in which it is decided not to perform a primary anastomosis, particular care must be taken not to leave the distal bowel stump in the abdominal cavity, since the poor general conditions of these patients may predispose to the easiness of the stump's closure.

The surgeon addresses a short stenosis in the proximal small intestine by making a longitudinal incision through all bowel layers at the stricture site. The incision is then closed transversely with separate reabsorbable stitches, potentially performing multiple strictureplasties simultaneously.

The surgeon performs Finney's technique for treating longer strictures, creating an enterotomy and opening a healthy bowel loop adjacent to the stenosed segment. A mechanical suture is applied, mimicking a lateral-lateral anastomosis.
It should be noted that the patient can present multiple strictures and all of them should be treated during surgery.

Finally, we must remember that these patients must be managed in a multidisciplinary way during the postoperative period, performing a video colonoscopy after six months to detect early relapses of the disease that make the intensification of medical treatment necessary.
